tracks.title: Module 1 · Getting started
tracks.summary: Install Total.js, meet the tools, and give Tasker its first endpoint.

lessons.title: Welcome — let's build Tasker
lessons.theory:
# Welcome to TotalCampus 👋

You won't read documentation here. You'll **build a real app**.

## Meet *Tasker*

Throughout this course we'll build **Tasker**, a small but real **task manager**: create tasks, mark them done, and list them. We start with a single line that says *hello*, and by the end Tasker has a real API, a database, validation, background jobs and even a simple web page.

Every new concept shows up exactly when Tasker needs it — so it always makes sense.

## How each lesson works

1. **Goal** — what you'll be able to do.
2. **Learn** — the idea, explained simply.
3. **Build it in Tasker** — we add it to the app.
4. **Practice** — a hands-on challenge you run right here.

## The road ahead

1. **Getting started** — install Total.js and serve Tasker's first response.
2. **Anatomy** — organize Tasker into the right folders.
3. **Routing & controllers** — build Tasker's full API.
4. **Working with data** — describe and validate a task.
5. **Databases** — store tasks for real.
6. **Toolbox** — config, utilities, background jobs.
7. **Users & sharing** — accounts, login, and tasks per user.
8. **Real-time & views** — a web page for Tasker, with live updates.

Ready? Let's go. 🚀

## 💡 Why Total.js?

One `npm install total5` and you're done — Total.js has **zero npm dependencies**. No 300-package `node_modules`, no dependency tree to audit, no version conflicts. The whole framework is a few megabytes, starts in milliseconds, and runs with a tiny memory footprint.

lessons.title: A little JavaScript
lessons.theory:
# A little JavaScript

> **Goal:** brush up the JavaScript we'll use constantly to build Tasker.

Total.js is **pure JavaScript**, so a little goes a long way. Here's the toolbox.

## Learn

**Variables** hold values:

```js
const name = 'Tasker';   // can't be reassigned
let count = 0;           // can change
```

**Objects** are perfect for a single task — a bundle of named values:

```js
const task = { id: 1, title: 'Buy milk', done: false };
console.log(task.title);   // Buy milk
```

**Arrays** are perfect for a list of tasks:

```js
const tasks = [task, { id: 2, title: 'Walk the dog', done: true }];
console.log(tasks.length); // 2
```

**Functions** wrap reusable logic:

```js
function isDone(task) {
    return task.done === true;
}
```

## Build it in Tasker

Tasker is, at heart, **an array of task objects** and some functions that work on them. You just met both. Time to make one.
lessons.solutionCode:
var task = { id: 1, title: 'Buy milk', done: false };
console.log(task.title);

challenge.prompt: Create a `task` object with a `title` of **Buy milk**, then log its title.

lessons.title: Meet Node.js
lessons.theory:
# Meet Node.js

> **Goal:** understand the engine that will run Tasker.

## Learn

**Node.js** runs JavaScript **outside the browser** — on a server, from the terminal. Tasker is a Node.js app.

Node comes with **npm**, the package manager. You install libraries (including Total.js) with one command:

```bash
$ npm install total5
```

On the web, data travels as **JSON** — text that looks just like a JavaScript object. You'll convert between the two all the time:

```js
const text = JSON.stringify({ title: 'Buy milk' }); // object -> JSON text
const obj  = JSON.parse(text);                       // JSON text -> object
```

## Build it in Tasker

When Tasker answers a request, it sends its tasks **as JSON**. So before we send anything, let's turn a tasks array into JSON text — exactly what the server will do for us later.

> In TotalCampus there's no real terminal: you practice the **language and logic** here, and the real Node commands come alive when you run a project in Total.js Code.
lessons.solutionCode:
var tasks = [{ id: 1, title: 'Buy milk', done: false }];
console.log(JSON.stringify(tasks));

challenge.prompt: Build a `tasks` array with at least one task and print it as JSON using `JSON.stringify`, so the output shows **Buy milk**.

lessons.title: What is Total.js, and why?
lessons.theory:
# Why Total.js?

> **Goal:** understand what Total.js is and why we'll build Tasker with it.

## Learn

Total.js is a **full-stack framework** for Node.js, written in **pure JavaScript**. One framework covers your **server, your API, and your client UI** — no glue code, no dependency soup.

Why it's a great fit for Tasker (and everything else):

- **Zero dependencies** — the core ships with no third-party packages. Less to break, less to audit.
- **Batteries included** — routing, schemas, validation, a query builder, a view engine, WebSockets, email… all built in. We'll use most of them on Tasker.
- **Full-stack** — the same ideas on the backend and on the frontend (the UI library / jComponent).
- **Fast & light** — built for performance and low memory from day one.
- **Convention over configuration** — you drop files into the right folders and the framework wires everything for you. (That's Module 2.)

## Build it in Tasker

Everything we add to Tasker — routes, schemas, the database, the web page — comes from Total.js itself. No extra packages to hunt down.

> Is it *the best* framework? We might be a little biased 😉 — but by the end you'll judge for yourself, having built a real app. Next: let's install it and start Tasker's server.

lessons.title: Install Total.js & run your first server
lessons.theory:
# Install Total.js & run your first server

> **Goal:** install Total.js and serve Tasker's first response.

## Learn

In a real terminal you'd create a folder and install Total.js from npm:

```bash
$ npm install total5
```

Every Total.js app starts from a small **`index.js`**:

```js
require('total5');

ROUTE('GET /', function($) {
    $.json({ app: 'Tasker', status: 'running' });
});

Total.http({ load: 'none' });
```

- `require('total5')` loads the framework.
- `ROUTE('GET /', fn)` connects a URL to a function.
- `$` is the **controller** — your handle on the request, and how you respond (`$.json(...)` sends JSON).
- `Total.http(...)` starts the web server.

Run it with `node index.js` and open `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.

## Build it in Tasker

Let's give Tasker a front page that reports it's alive. Register `GET /` and respond with a small JSON status.

> Here in TotalCampus we **simulate** the server: register a route and we'll call it for you, no setup required.

challenge.prompt: Register `GET /` so Tasker responds with JSON where `app` is **Tasker**.

lessons.title: Your first endpoint: the task list

challenge.prompt: Create `GET /tasks` that returns a JSON list of tasks including one titled **Buy milk**.

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory: # Recap > A calm look back — and the other tools Total.js gives you for the same job, no extra packages. ## What you built You installed Total.js, ran a server, and gave Tasker its first endpoints (`GET /`, `GET /tasks`). ## What you used `ROUTE` to define routes, the controller `$`, `$.json(...)` to respond, and `Total.http(...)` to start the server. ## Other ways to do it (all built in) A controller can answer in many shapes — pick the right one: - `$.json(data)` — JSON (APIs). - `$.view('name', model)` — an HTML page. - `$.html('Hi')` / `$.plain('text')` — raw HTML or text. - `$.success()` / `$.invalid('msg')` — the "it worked / it failed" convention. - `$.file(path)` / `$.image(...)` — send files or dynamic images. ## 🔎 More to explore `UID()` / `GUID()` for unique ids, `NOW` for the current time, `CONF` for settings — all global, all ready.

tracks.title: Module 2 · Anatomy of a Total.js project
tracks.summary: Organize Tasker into the right folders and learn what each one is for.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 2 · Organize Tasker

> **Goal:** learn how a Total.js project is laid out — by tidying up Tasker.

Right now all of Tasker lives in one file. That's fine for a toy, but real apps grow. Total.js keeps things tidy with one simple rule:

> **Put each kind of file in its folder, and the framework wires everything for you.**

In this module you'll meet that folder **map**, then move Tasker's routes into their proper place — no more giant `index.js`.

Once you know where things go, every later module just drops new files into the right folder. Let's tidy up.

## 💡 Why Total.js?

That folder convention means **no glue code and no wiring** to assemble your app. Other frameworks leave the structure (and a pile of plugins) up to you; Total.js gives you a proven layout where things just work when they're in the right place.

lessons.title: The project map
lessons.theory:
# The project map

> **Goal:** know what every folder in a Total.js project is for.

## Learn

A Total.js project is just a set of **folders**, each with one job. They're all optional — you use what you need, when you need it.

**The folders you'll use in Tasker:**

- **`/controllers`** — routes: the URLs your app answers (Tasker's API).
- **`/schemas`** — the shape and validation of your data (a Task).
- **`/databases`** — stored data (TextDB files).
- **`/views`** — HTML pages (Tasker's web page).
- **`/public`** — static files served as-is: CSS, images, client JS.
- **`/definitions`** — code that runs once at startup.
- **`/resources`** — text for translations.
- **`/modules`** and **`/middleware`** — reusable pieces and request filters (later on).

**Plus two files in the project root:**

- **`index.js`** — the start script (you met it in Module 1).
- **`config`** — your app's settings.

## Loading order

At boot, Total.js loads these in a fixed order (`config` → `resources` → `schemas` → `definitions` → `controllers` → …), so by the time a request arrives, **everything is ready**.

## Build it in Tasker

You don't need to memorize this. Just remember the idea: **each Tasker feature has a home.** Next we'll create Tasker's first real home — the `controllers` folder.

lessons.title: Your first controller

challenge.prompt: Inside `exports.install`, register `GET /tasks` so it returns a list including a task titled **Buy milk**.
challenge.hint: Put your ROUTE('GET /tasks', ...) inside the exports.install function.

lessons.title: Settings & startup
lessons.theory:
# Settings & startup

> **Goal:** know where Tasker's settings and startup code live.

Two more pieces complete the picture.

## The `config` file

Your app's settings live in a plain **`config`** file in the project root, written as `key : value` pairs:

```config
name        : Tasker
version     : 1.0.0
```

You read them anywhere through the global **`CONF`** object (`CONF.name`). We'll use it properly in Module 6.

## `.env`

Secrets and per-machine values (database passwords, ports) go in a **`.env`** file, loaded automatically into `process.env`. Keep it out of version control.

## `/definitions` — run once at startup

Files in **`/definitions`** run a single time when the app boots. Use them for one-off setup: connecting to services, seeding data, registering global helpers.

## Tasker's home, mapped

You now know where every piece of Tasker will live. From here, each module fills a folder:

- **Module 3** — flesh out `/controllers` with the full task API.
- **Module 4** — add a Task **schema** in `/schemas`.
- **Module 5** — store tasks in `/databases`.
- **Module 7** — give Tasker a **view** in `/views`.

Onward! 🚀

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> What you organized — and the other places Total.js lets things live.

## What you built
You tidied Tasker into folders and moved its routes into a **controller** (`exports.install`).

## What you used
`/controllers`, the `config` file, and an idea of `/definitions`.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
Every folder is optional and each has a job you can lean on:

- `/schemas` — data that can **auto-route** itself as an API.
- `/definitions` — run-once startup code; register globals with `DEF`, shared data with `REPO`, shared functions with `FUNC`.
- `/modules` — reusable code shared across the app.
- `/middleware` — filters that run before routes.
- `/public` — static files served as-is.

## 🔎 More to explore
`IMPORT(url)` pulls in remote code, and `LOADCONFIG` / `LOADRESOURCE` load settings and translations on the fly.

tracks.title: Module 3 · Routing & controllers
tracks.summary: Build Tasker's full REST API — list, read, create, complete and delete tasks.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 3 · Build Tasker's API

> **Goal:** turn Tasker into a real API that can list, read, create, complete and delete tasks.

In Module 2 you created the `tasks` controller with one route. Now we'll fill it out into a complete **REST API** — the five things almost every app needs:

- **List** all tasks — `GET /tasks`
- **Read** one task — `GET /tasks/{id}`
- **Create** a task — `POST /tasks`
- **Complete** a task — `PUT /tasks/{id}`
- **Delete** a task — `DELETE /tasks/{id}`

By the end, Tasker's API is fully usable. Let's build it route by route. 🛠️

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Routing, body parsing, query parsing and the controller are **all built in**. In Express you'd add `body-parser`, a router, maybe `cors`… here it's one `ROUTE` and a `$`. Less to install, less to break, less to keep updated.

## 🔎 Good to know

Two routing superpowers for later:

- **API routing** — map one endpoint to many schema actions: `ROUTE('API /api/ -tasks_list *Tasks --> list')`. The client calls a single `/api/` URL and names the operation it wants. Perfect for SPAs.
- **Middleware** — reusable filters that run before a route: `MIDDLEWARE('logged', fn)`, then `ROUTE('+GET /tasks', ['logged'], handler)`.

lessons.title: Methods & the controller

challenge.prompt: Inside `exports.install`, register `GET /tasks` to return the shared `tasks` array (which includes **Buy milk**).

lessons.title: Reading one task: route parameters

challenge.prompt: Add `GET /tasks/{id}` that returns the matching task. Calling `GET /tasks/1` should return **Buy milk**.

lessons.title: Creating a task: reading the body

challenge.prompt: Add `POST /tasks` that reads `$.body.title` and returns the new task. We send `{ "title": "Read a book" }`, so the response must contain **Read a book**.
challenge.hint: var task = { id: Date.now(), title: $.body.title, done: false }; $.success(task);

lessons.title: Completing & deleting tasks

challenge.prompt: Add `PUT /tasks/{id}` that marks the matching task as done (`task.done = true`) and returns it. `PUT /tasks/1` should return the **Buy milk** task.
challenge.hint: Find the task by $.params.id, set task.done = true, then $.success(task).

lessons.title: Protecting routes: auth & sessions
lessons.theory:
# Protecting routes: auth & sessions

> **Goal:** make sure only the right people can touch Tasker's data.

## Learn

Right now anyone can call Tasker's API. Real apps need **authentication** (who are you?) and **authorization** (what may you do?).

Total.js handles this with an **`AUTH`** handler that runs before protected routes and decides who the user is:

```js
// in a /definitions file — runs for authorized requests
AUTH(function($) {
    var token = $.headers['x-token'];
    var user = findUserByToken(token);   // your lookup
    if (user)
        $.success(user);   // attaches the user to $.user
    else
        $.invalid(401);    // not logged in
});
```

Then mark a route as **authorized** with a `+`:

```js
ROUTE('+GET /tasks', list);   // requires a logged-in user
ROUTE('GET /about', about);   // public
```

Inside a protected handler, the current user is on **`$.user`**:

```js
ROUTE('+GET /tasks', function($) {
    console.log('Requested by', $.user.name);
    $.json(tasks);
});
```

- `AUTH` runs once per request and decides who you are.
- `+` marks routes that **require** authentication.
- `$.user` is the authenticated user inside the route.
- `$.invalid(401)` / `$.invalid(403)` reject unauthenticated / forbidden requests.

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Sessions, the auth flow and route guards are **built into routing** — no `passport`, no `express-session`, no extra middleware stack to assemble. One `AUTH` handler and a `+` on your routes.

## Build it in Tasker

To make Tasker's tasks private you'd add an `AUTH` handler, mark the task routes with `+`, and read the owner from `$.user`.

> This one's a read-through: auth needs a real browser session to try out, so there's no challenge here. You've got the full picture of how to lock Tasker down.

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> Your API is complete — here's everything routing can also do.

## What you built
A full REST API (list, read, create, update, delete) plus **authenticated** routes.

## What you used
`ROUTE` with `GET/POST/PUT/DELETE`, `{id}` params, `$.params` / `$.body` / `$.query`, `$.success` / `$.invalid`, and `AUTH` + `$.user`.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **API routing** — one endpoint, many operations: `ROUTE('API /api/ -tasks_list *Tasks --> list')`.
- **File routing** and **WebSocket routing** (`ROUTE('SOCKET /...')`).
- **`MIDDLEWARE`** for cross-cutting logic (logging, auth) applied per route.
- **`CORS()`** to allow browser apps from other origins — built in, no package.
- **`BLOCKED`** to ban IPs, **`UNAUTHORIZED`** helpers, and error routes (`ROUTE('#404', ...)`).

## 🔎 More to explore
On `$` you also get `$.cookie`, `$.header`, `$.redirect`, `$.throw404()`, and `$.successful(...)`.

tracks.title: Module 4 · Working with data
tracks.summary: Give Tasker's data a shape and validate it with schemas and actions.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 4 · Give Tasker's data a shape

> **Goal:** make sure the data flowing through Tasker is always valid.

Right now Tasker's `POST /tasks` would happily accept `{ }` — a task with no title. That's a bug waiting to happen. Real apps **validate** their data.

In this module you'll:

1. **Describe** a Task with a **schema** — its fields and which are required.
2. Attach **actions** (your business logic) to that schema.
3. Get **validation for free** — Total.js rejects bad input before your code runs.

Let's give Tasker a backbone. 🦴

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Schema **validation is part of the core**. Elsewhere you'd reach for `joi`, `zod` or `class-validator` and wire them up by hand. In Total.js, marking a field `required` is all it takes — every request is validated for you.

lessons.title: Schemas: describe your data
lessons.theory:
# Schemas: describe your data

> **Goal:** describe what a Task looks like with a schema.

## Learn

A **schema** declares the shape of your data: its fields, their types, and which are required. You create one with **`NEWSCHEMA`**:

```js
NEWSCHEMA('Task', function(schema) {
    schema.define('title', String, true);   // required
    schema.define('done', Boolean);         // optional
});
```

`schema.define(name, type, required)`:
- **name** — the field name.
- **type** — `String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Date`…
- **required** — pass `true` to make it mandatory.

That third argument is the magic: any field marked `true` **must** be present, or Total.js rejects the input. No `if` checks needed.

## Build it in Tasker

Describe a Task: a **required** `title` (String) and an optional `done` (Boolean).

challenge.prompt: Define a `Task` schema with a **required** `title` (String) and an optional `done` (Boolean).
challenge.hint: schema.define('title', String, true); and schema.define('done', Boolean);

lessons.title: Actions & validation

challenge.prompt: Inside `POST /tasks`, run the `Task/insert` action with `$.body`. A valid body returns the task; an empty body must be rejected by validation.

lessons.title: Reusable logic: operations
lessons.theory:
# Reusable logic: operations & tasks

> **Goal:** know where logic lives when it isn't tied to one schema.

## Learn

Schema actions are perfect for "do something with a Task". But some logic is **shared** across the whole app — sending a notification, generating a report, cleaning up old data. For that, Total.js gives you:

- **`NEWOPERATION('name', fn)`** — a standalone unit of logic you can run from anywhere.
- **`NEWACTION('Group/name', { action })`** — like a schema action, but not bound to a schema.
- **Tasks** — chain several steps into one resumable workflow.

```js
NEWOPERATION('notify', function($) {
    // e.g. send an email, log an event...
    $.success();
});
```

## Build it in Tasker

A simple rule of thumb to keep Tasker tidy:

- Logic about **a Task** → a **schema action** (`Task/insert`, `Task/list`…).
- Logic **shared** across the app → an **operation**.

That's data sorted. Next module we stop faking it — Tasker gets a real **database**. 🗄️

lessons.title: One endpoint, many operations
lessons.summary: API routing — the door for SPAs
lessons.theory:
# One endpoint, many operations

> **Goal:** expose your schema actions through a single API endpoint.

## Learn

The REST routes from Module 3 (`GET /tasks`, `POST /tasks`…) are great. But Total.js is famous for a second style — **API routing**: *one* URL, many named operations, each wired to a schema action.

```js
ROUTE('API /api/ -tasks_list      *Tasks --> list');
ROUTE('API /api/ -tasks_read/{id} *Tasks --> read');
ROUTE('API /api/ +tasks_insert    *Tasks --> insert');
```

Read each line as: *at `/api/`, the operation **`tasks_list`** runs the **`list`** action of the **`Tasks`** schema.*

- **`-`** = public, **`+`** = must be logged in (the same auth flow from Module 3).
- The client names the operation it wants; the framework validates the input and routes it to the action.

### Why this shape?

It's built for **single-page apps**. The browser talks to one endpoint and asks for operations by name. In the frontend course you'll call these with Total.js's **`TAPI()`** (over HTTP) and **`WAPI()`** (over a WebSocket) helpers — no URLs to hardcode, no `fetch` boilerplate.

> 💡 REST and API routing happily coexist. Use REST for simple resources, API routing when a rich client drives everything through one door.

## Build it in Tasker

The `Tasks` schema and its `list` action are ready. Expose `list` as the API operation **`tasks_list`** at `/api/`. (Here we call it by POSTing `{ "schema": "tasks_list" }` — exactly what `TAPI('tasks_list')` sends for you.)

challenge.prompt: Declare an API route so the operation `tasks_list` at `/api/` runs the `Tasks` schema's `list` action. Calling it should return the tasks.

checks.title: Calling the operation returns the tasks

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> Your data has a shape — and Total.js has more ways to organize logic.

## What you built
A `Task` **schema** with validation, and a schema **action** wired to a route.

## What you used
`NEWSCHEMA`, `schema.define(field, type, required)`, `schema.action`, `$.action(...)`, and automatic validation.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **`NEWACTION('Group/name', ...)`** — actions not tied to a schema.
- **`NEWOPERATION` + `EXEC`/`ACTION`** — reusable units of logic you run from anywhere.
- **`NEWTASK`** — chain steps into one resumable workflow.
- **JSON Schema** validation and **`ErrorBuilder`** for rich, localized errors.

## 🔎 More to explore
Schemas can set **defaults**, **transform** values, and nest other schemas — declarative data handling without a single helper library.

tracks.title: Module 5 · Databases
tracks.summary: Store Tasker's tasks for real with QueryBuilder — insert, find, filter and update.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 5 · Store Tasker's data

> **Goal:** stop faking it — give Tasker a real database.

So far Tasker's tasks have lived in a plain array. Restart the app and they vanish. Real apps **persist** their data.

In this module you'll meet **QueryBuilder**, Total.js's database API, and use it to:

1. **Insert** and **read** records.
2. **Filter** with `where`.
3. Rewire Tasker's routes to the database instead of the array.

The best part: the routes barely change. Let's make Tasker's tasks real. 🗄️

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Total.js ships with **QueryBuilder and TextDB** built in — you can build and ship a real app with persistence **without installing a single database package**. Move to PostgreSQL later and the same QueryBuilder code still works. No ORM, no driver juggling.

challenge.prompt: Insert two tasks (one pending, one done), then use `.where('done', false)` to log only the **pending** ones — so **Buy milk** appears.

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> Tasker persists data — and you can store it many ways without extra packages.

## What you built
Tasker's tasks now live in a database, read and written with QueryBuilder.

## What you used
`DB()` / `DATA`, `find` / `read` / `insert` / `update` / `remove`, `.where(...)`, and callbacks.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **TextDB / NoSQL** (`NOSQL`) — the built-in database; zero install.
- **PostgreSQL** via `querybuilderpg` — **same QueryBuilder methods**, so you switch databases without refactoring.
- **`CACHE`**, **`FILECACHE`**, **`MEMORIZE`** — in-memory and file caching for hot data.
- **`FILESTORAGE`** — store files and blobs.
- **`BACKUP`** / **`RESTORE`** — back up and restore your data.

## 🔎 More to explore
`NEWDB` registers custom connections, and QueryBuilder also does `sort`, `take`/`skip` (paging), `fields`, and joins.

tracks.title: Module 6 · The framework toolbox
tracks.summary: Polish Tasker with configuration, utilities and graceful error handling.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 6 · Tasker's toolbox

> **Goal:** polish Tasker with the tools Total.js gives you out of the box.

Tasker works — but real apps need a few more things: **settings** you can tweak without touching code, **helpers** for everyday jobs, and **graceful errors** when something goes wrong.

In this module you'll:

1. Read app **configuration** with `CONF`.
2. Use built-in **utilities** (`U.*`) — like generating ids.
3. Handle **errors** cleanly with `$.invalid`.
4. Meet **background jobs** and the rest of the standard library.

Total.js bundles all of this, so you rarely need extra packages. Let's polish Tasker. ✨

## 💡 Why Total.js?

This is where zero-dependency really shows. **All of this is in the core**, no extra packages: email (SMTP), image processing, a REST client, scheduling/CRON, encryption, hashing, CSS/JS/HTML minification, file storage, Markdown, XML parsing, LDAP, workers… A typical Node stack pulls in `nodemailer`, `sharp`, `axios`, `node-cron`, `bcrypt`, `marked`… Total.js maintains it all as **one tested unit**.

## 🔎 Good to know

The toolbox runs deep — all built in, no packages:

- **Scheduling / reminders** — `CRON('0 9 * * *', fn)` runs a job on a schedule (e.g. email pending-task reminders every morning).
- **Proxy** — forward routes to another service: `PROXY('/api/', 'https://other-service')`.
- **Run Python** — Total.js can execute Python scripts (and `pip install`) out of the box via the `$python` config.
- **Run system commands** — `F.Child.exec('ls -la', callback)` shells out to the OS when you need it.

Total.js really does include the kitchen sink. 🧰

lessons.title: Configuration
lessons.theory:
# Configuration

> **Goal:** keep Tasker's settings out of the code with `CONF`.

## Learn

Things like your app's name, a page size, or an API key shouldn't be hard-coded. They live in a plain **`config`** file in the project root:

```config
name        : Tasker
version     : 1.0.0
pagelimit   : 20
```

…and you read them anywhere through the global **`CONF`** object:

```js
console.log(CONF.name);       // Tasker
console.log(CONF.pagelimit);  // 20
```

Change the file, and every part of your app sees the new value — no code changes. (Secrets like passwords go in `.env` instead.)

## Build it in Tasker

Give Tasker an `about` endpoint that reports its name and version from `CONF`.

challenge.prompt: Add `GET /about` that returns Tasker's name from `CONF.name` (set to **Tasker**) and its version.

lessons.title: Utilities
lessons.theory:
# Utilities

> **Goal:** stop reinventing common helpers — use `U.*`.

## Learn

Total.js ships a big box of utility functions on the global **`U`**:

```js
U.GUID(10);      // a random unique id, e.g. "k3f9ab2c7q"
U.random(100);   // a random number 0–100
'total js'.capitalize();  // "Total Js"
'My Task!'.slug();        // "my-task"
```

These save you from pulling in extra packages for everyday jobs.

## Build it in Tasker

Until now Tasker faked task ids. Let's give every new task a **real unique id** with `U.GUID` when it's created.

challenge.prompt: In `POST /tasks`, create the task with a `U.GUID()` id and return it. We send `{ "title": "Buy milk" }`.
challenge.hint: var task = { id: U.GUID(10), title: $.body.title, done: false }; $.success(task);

checks.title: You generate an id with U.GUID

lessons.title: Handling errors

challenge.prompt: In `POST /tasks`, if `$.body.title` is missing, reply with `$.invalid('Title is required')`. Otherwise return the task. An empty body must be rejected; `{ "title": "Buy milk" }` must be accepted.

lessons.title: Audit logs: who did what

challenge.prompt: Implement `DEF.onAudit` to handle audit entries (log the `message` and `username`), then call `AUDIT($, 'Task created')`. The output should show **Task created** by **Ada**.
challenge.hint: DEF.onAudit = function(name, log) { console.log(log.message, log.username); }; then AUDIT($, 'Task created');

lessons.title: Background jobs & the rest of the toolbox
Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> The core is *huge*. Here's the toolbox you can reach for — none of it needs a package.

## What you built
You added configuration, utilities and graceful error handling to Tasker.

## What you used
`CONF`, the `U.*` helpers, and `$.invalid`.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
Total.js bundles what other stacks install separately:

- **Email** — `MAIL`, `HTMLMAIL`, `LOGMAIL`.
- **HTTP client** — `RESTBuilder`, `AJAX`, `REQUEST`.
- **Images** — `Image` (resize, crop, convert).
- **Scheduling** — `CRON('0 9 * * *', fn)` for reminders and jobs.
- **Caching** — `CACHE`, `FILECACHE`, `MEMORIZE`.
- **Crypto** — `ENCRYPT`, `DECRYPT`, `HASH`.
- **Proxy** — `PROXY('/api/', 'https://service')`.
- **Run Python** — `PYPELINE` / the `$python` config.
- **Run system commands** — `SHELL`, `EXEC`, `F.Child.exec(...)`.
- **Events & pub/sub** — `ON`, `EMIT`, `PUBLISH`, `SUBSCRIBE`.
- **Threads / workers** — `NEWTHREAD`, `NEWTHREADPOOL`.
- Plus **LDAP**, **AUDIT** logging, **CORS**, minification, even **AI models** (`AIMODEL`) and `NPMINSTALL` at runtime.

## 🔎 More to explore
That's the point of zero-dependency: one tested framework instead of a tower of `node_modules`.

tracks.title: Module 8 · Views & real-time
tracks.summary: Give Tasker a face: an HTML page with Tangular and live updates over WebSockets.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 8 · Give Tasker a face

> **Goal:** turn Tasker's API into something people can see and use live.

So far Tasker speaks only JSON. Now we give it a **web page** — and make it **update live** as tasks change.

In this module you'll:

1. Meet the **view engine** and write a simple HTML page with **Tangular**.
2. **Render** that page from a route, passing it the tasks.
3. Push **live updates** to the browser with **WebSockets**.

> Pages and sockets run in a browser, not in our sandbox — so most of this module is *write the code*, and we check it for you. The one part we can run is wiring a view to a route.

Let's give Tasker a face. 🎨

## 💡 Why Total.js?

The **view engine (Tangular)** and a full **WebSocket server** are built in — no `ejs`/`pug`, no `ws`/`socket.io`. Server-rendered HTML and real-time push come from the same framework that serves your API: one tool, one mental model.

## 🔎 Good to know

Real-time + the toolbox = powerful combos:

- **Broadcast** — push an update to *every* connected client at once (complete a task, and all open tabs refresh).
- **Notifications** — pair WebSockets with the built-in **Mail** to alert a user by email.

Imagine *users sending each other tasks*: a live socket update for whoever's online, an email for whoever isn't — all from one framework. (We'll build exactly this in a future module.)

lessons.title: Views & Tangular
lessons.summary: HTML pages with the view engine
lessons.theory:
# Views & Tangular > **Goal:** write an HTML page that shows Tasker's tasks. ## Learn HTML pages live in the **`/views`** folder. Total.js renders them with its template engine, **Tangular**, which mixes HTML with `@{...}` expressions: ```html

@{CONF.name}

    @{foreach m in model}
  • @{m.title}
    • @{end}
``` - `@{CONF.name}` — prints a value. - `@{foreach m in model}` … `@{end}` — loops over the data the route gives the view (`model`). - `@{m.title}` — prints each item's field. Total.js fills in the data and sends finished HTML to the browser. ## Build it in Tasker Write a `tasks` view that lists every task's title. Loop over `model` with `@{foreach}` and print `@{m.title}`.
lessons.starterCode: @{CONF.name} — tasks
lessons.solutionCode:
@{CONF.name} — tasks

    @{foreach m in model}
  • @{m.title}
    • @{end}

challenge.prompt: Write the view: loop over the tasks with `@{foreach m in model}` and show each `@{m.title}`.

lessons.summary: Wire a view to a route with $.view
lessons.theory:
# Rendering the page

> **Goal:** serve Tasker's page from a route, filled with real tasks.

## Learn

A route turns data into a page with **`$.view`**:

```js
ROUTE('GET /', function($) {
    var tasks = [ /* ...from the database... */ ];
    $.view('tasks', tasks);   // render /views/tasks.html with this data
});
```

- The first argument is the **view name** (the file in `/views`).
- The second is the **model** — the data the view loops over (`@{foreach m in model}`).

So the same tasks that power your API now power your web page. One source of truth, two faces.

## Build it in Tasker

Make `GET /` render the `tasks` view with the tasks list.

challenge.prompt: In `GET /`, render the `tasks` view passing the tasks as the model with `$.view('tasks', tasks)`.

checks.title: The page is built from the tasks

challenge.prompt: Register `SOCKET /live` and, on the `open` event, send the client a message with `client.send`.

checks.title: You push a message to the client

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> Tasker has a face and a live channel — and Total.js has more ways to present and push.

## What you built
A Tangular **view** rendered from a route, and a **WebSocket** route for live updates.

## What you used
`$.view('name', model)`, Tangular (`@{...}`, `@{foreach}`), `SOCKET` routes, `$.on`, and `client.send`.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- Render with `$.view`, `$.html`, or `TEMPLATE(...)`; views support **layouts, partials and helpers**.
- **Broadcast** to every connected client at once.
- **`WEBSOCKETCLIENT`** — connect *out* to another server's socket.
- Combine sockets with **`MAIL`** and **`PUBLISH`/`SUBSCRIBE`** for notifications (online → socket, offline → email).
- **`TMSCLIENT`** — message between separate apps.

## 🔎 More to explore
`DOWNLOAD(url)` streams files, and `$.image(...)` generates images on the fly.

tracks.title: Module 10 · Going to production
tracks.summary: Ship Tasker: release mode, bundles, deploying and scaling.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 10 · Ship Tasker

> **Goal:** take Tasker from your laptop to the world.

Tasker is built — API, validation, database, a web page, live updates. The last step is **shipping** it.

In this module you'll learn how to:

1. Run Tasker in **release mode**.
2. Package it as a **bundle**.
3. **Deploy** and scale it.

Then we'll look back at everything you've built. Let's go live. 🚀

## 💡 Why Total.js?

**Bundles** and **clustering** are part of the framework, not add-ons. Ship your whole app as one file and scale across every CPU core with one line — no PM2, no extra process manager. Low memory, high throughput, by design.

lessons.title: Release mode
lessons.theory:
# Release mode

> **Goal:** run Tasker the way a server should.

## Learn

In development you run `node index.js`. For production, add **`--release`**:

```bash
$ node index.js --release
```

Release mode turns on caching and optimizations and turns off debug features — Tasker runs faster and leaner.

A few start-script options you'll meet (in `index.js`):

```js
options.release = process.argv.includes('--release');
// options.port = 8000;
// options.cluster = 'auto';   // use every CPU core
```

## Environments

Keep settings per environment with **`.env`** and the `config` file:

- secrets (database URLs, API keys) → `.env`, never committed.
- tweakable values (app name, page size) → `config`, read via `CONF`.

Same code, different settings — that's how one Tasker runs on your laptop and on a server.

lessons.title: Bundles
lessons.summary: Package the whole app in one file
lessons.theory:
# Bundles

> **Goal:** package Tasker for distribution.

## Learn

A **bundle** is a single file that contains a whole Total.js app — controllers, schemas, views, everything. It's how Total.js ships apps in release mode.

Why bundles are handy:

- **One artifact** to copy to your server.
- **Layering**: a bundle can extend or override another, so you can ship a base app and customize it per client.
- **Clean deploys**: no `node_modules` juggling for your own code.

You build and run bundles with the framework's tooling; the result is a tidy, self-contained Tasker ready to drop onto a server.

> Bundles shine when you distribute the same app to many places — exactly what the Total.js ecosystem (CMS, OpenPlatform…) does.

lessons.title: Deploy, scale & what's next
lessons.summary: Go live — and where to go from here
lessons.theory:
# Deploy, scale & what's next

> **Goal:** put Tasker online, and plan your next steps.

## Deploy

A Total.js app is a normal Node.js process, so hosting is simple:

- Run it behind a **reverse proxy** (like NGINX) for TLS and a clean domain.
- Use **clustering** (`options.cluster = 'auto'`) to use every CPU core.
- Host it anywhere that runs Node — a VPS, a container, or **Total.js Cloud**.

## Tasker is live 🎉

Look back: from an empty folder you built a complete, production-shaped backend — a running server, a tidy structure, a full REST API, validation, a real database, config and utilities, a live web page, and a path to production. You understand every piece.

## One more level

Tasker runs on one process today. When you need to serve thousands of users — or bend the framework to your own will — there's a final step.

Ready to **scale across every CPU core** and **extend the framework itself**? That's **Module 11 · Going expert**. 👑

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> Shipping, the Total.js way — batteries included.

## What you built
You learned release mode, bundles, and how to deploy and scale Tasker.

## What you used
`--release`, bundles, and `options.cluster`.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **Clustering** — `options.cluster = 'auto'` plus `F.Cluster`; self-healing workers, no PM2.
- **Unix sockets** for fast reverse-proxy setups.
- **`BACKUP`** / **`RESTORE`** for data, **`AUDIT`** for an audit trail.
- Environments via `.env` and `CONF`; **watchers** and **livereload** in development.

## 🔎 More to explore
Run anywhere Node runs — a VPS, a container, or **Total.js Cloud** — with the same single artifact.

tracks.title: Module 11 · Going expert
tracks.summary: Scale Tasker across CPU cores and extend the framework itself.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 11 · Going expert

> **Goal:** scale Tasker across many cores and bend the framework to your will.

You've built and shipped Tasker. This module is for when you outgrow a single process and want **full control** of the framework itself.

You'll learn to:

1. **Cluster** Tasker across every CPU core.
2. **Extend** the framework with your own dynamic extensions.
3. See where the wider **Total.js platform** can take you.

Welcome to the deep end. 👑

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Clustering and live extensibility are **core features**, not third-party bolt-ons. You get production-grade scaling and a plugin system out of the box — the same foundation the entire Total.js platform is built on.

lessons.title: Clustering
lessons.theory:
# Clustering

> **Goal:** run Tasker on every CPU core for maximum throughput.

## Learn

Node.js uses a single core by default. To use the whole machine, Total.js can run your app as a **cluster** — several worker processes ("forks") behind one port — with a single option in `index.js`:

```js
options.cluster = 'auto';   // one worker per CPU core
options.limit = 10;         // cap the workers (optional)

Total.run(options);
```

- `'auto'` spawns one worker per CPU core (or pass a number).
- A **primary** process spreads incoming requests across the workers.
- If a worker crashes or stalls, the framework **restarts it automatically** — no PM2, no external process manager.

Workers can talk to each other:

```js
ON('cluster', function(type, message) {
    // broadcast between workers — e.g. "a task changed"
});
```

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Clustering is **part of the core**, not an add-on. Each worker is a lean Node process, so Tasker scales across cores with **low memory per worker** and automatic self-healing — features that elsewhere mean extra tools and config.

## Build it in Tasker

Production-ready scaling is one line: set `options.cluster = 'auto'` in `index.js`, and Tasker uses the whole server.

lessons.title: Extending the framework
lessons.summary: Add behavior to a running app

lessons.title: The Total.js platform — and graduation 🎓
lessons.theory:
# The Total.js platform — and graduation 🎓

> **Goal:** see how far Total.js reaches, and celebrate what you built.

## Beyond the framework

Tasker is one app, but Total.js is a whole **platform** built on the same zero-dependency core:

- **FlowStream** — visual, event-driven pipelines.
- **UI Builder** — build interfaces visually.
- **OpenPlatform** — a foundation for multi-app portals.
- **CMS**, **Message Service (TMS)**, **Workers** — content, real-time messaging, background jobs.

All of it speaks the same language you just learned.

## You did it 👏

From an empty folder you built **Tasker** — a real, validated, persistent, real-time, production-ready, scalable, extensible Total.js application. And you understand every line.

You're not "learning Total.js" anymore. **You're a Total.js developer.**

## Where next

- Build for real in **Total.js Code**, without the sandbox's limits.
- Keep **docs.totaljs.com** close as a reference.
- Ready for the browser? The **Frontend aula** teaches the Total.js UI library (jComponent) the same way — by building.

Congratulations. 🚀

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> The deep end — and how far the same framework reaches.

## What you built
You learned to **cluster** Tasker across cores and **extend** it live with extensions.

## What you used
`options.cluster`, `ON('cluster', ...)`, and dynamic extensions (`exports.install`).

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **`PROXY` / `PROXYSERVER`** — build gateways and edge services.
- **`TMS` / `TMSCLIENT`** — real-time messaging between instances.
- **`NEWTHREADPOOL`** — offload heavy work to worker threads.
- **`FlowStream`** — event-driven pipelines, visual or in code.

## 🔎 The whole platform
FlowStream, UI Builder, OpenPlatform, CMS — all built on the same zero-dependency core you just mastered. You can go as deep as you like.

tracks.title: Module 7 · Users & sharing
tracks.summary: Accounts, login, and tasks that belong to a user.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 7 · Tasker goes multi-user

> **Goal:** turn Tasker from one shared list into an app with real accounts.

So far Tasker keeps a single pile of tasks. Real apps have **users** — each with their own login and their own tasks.

In this module you'll:

1. Give users a **shape** (a `User` schema) and let people **register**.
2. **Log in** — find the user and check their password.
3. Make every task **belong to a user**, so each person sees only their own.

This is the foundation for the big features ahead — sharing tasks between people, and reminders. Let's add accounts. 👥

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Schemas, validation, password hashing (`HASH`/`ENCRYPT`), sessions and the `AUTH` flow you'll lean on here are **all built in** — no `passport`, no `bcrypt` wiring, no session middleware to assemble. Accounts are just more Total.js.

lessons.title: Users have a shape too

challenge.prompt: Define the `User` schema (done for you) and wire `POST /users` to run `User/register`. Posting a name, email and password should create the user.

lessons.title: Logging in
lessons.summary: Find the user, check the password

challenge.prompt: Make `POST /login` look up the user by email and check the password. Return `{ id, name }` on success, or `$.invalid('Wrong email or password')` otherwise.
challenge.hint: DB().read('users').where('email', $.body.email).callback(function(err, user) { if (user && user.password === $.body.password) $.success({ id: user.id, name: user.name }); else $.invalid('Wrong email or password'); });

lessons.title: Whose tasks? Linking data to users

challenge.prompt: Make `GET /tasks` return only the current user's tasks by filtering with `.where('userid', current.id)`. The response should include **Buy milk** (user 1) but not **Read a book** (user 2).

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> A calm look back — and the other tools Total.js gives you for accounts, no extra packages.

## What you built
Tasker now has **accounts**: users can register and log in, and every task belongs to its owner.

## What you used
`NEWSCHEMA('User')`, `schema.action('register')`, `DB().read/find` with `.where('email'/'userid', ...)`, `$.success` / `$.invalid`, and `$.user` behind protected (`+`) routes.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **Passwords** — `HASH('sha256', value)`, `ENCRYPT` / `DECRYPT`; never store plain text.
- **Sessions** — `$.cookie(name, value, expire)` plus the `AUTH` delegate to populate `$.user`.
- **Corporate login** — `LDAP(...)` for Active Directory, all built in.
- **OAuth / SSO** — the `OpenAuth` flow for "log in with Google/GitHub".
- **Roles & permissions** — store roles on the user and gate routes with `ROUTE('+GET /admin/ @admin', ...)`.

## 🔎 What's next
Now that tasks have owners, users can **send tasks to each other** — a live WebSocket ping for whoever's online, an email for whoever isn't. We'll build it once you've added a live channel — that's **Module 9 · Sharing & notifications**. 📨

tracks.title: Module 9 · Sharing & notifications
tracks.summary: Assign tasks to people, notify them live or by email.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 9 · Tasker becomes collaborative

> **Goal:** let people **send tasks to each other** — and get notified.

Tasker has accounts now. The natural next step: Ada assigns a task to Beto, and Beto finds out **right away**.

But "right away" depends on Beto:

- If he's **online**, push it down his open **WebSocket** — instant, no refresh.
- If he's **offline**, send him an **email** so he sees it later.

In this module you'll:

1. **Assign** a task to another user.
2. **Notify online users** in real time over a socket.
3. Pick the right channel — **socket if online, email if not** — with one small function.

One framework, both channels. 🤝

## 💡 Why Total.js?

Real-time sockets *and* a full SMTP mail client are **both built in** — no `socket.io`, no `nodemailer`, no glue. The online/offline notification pattern you'll build here is something teams reach for constantly, and Total.js has every piece in the box.

lessons.title: Assigning a task to someone

challenge.prompt: Wire `POST /tasks/{id}/assign` to set `assignedto` from `$.body.userid` and return the updated task.

lessons.title: Notify online users in real time
One notify, two channels
lessons.summary: Socket if online, email if not
lessons.theory:
# One notify, two channels

> **Goal:** notify a user the best way available — socket if online, email if not.

## Learn

Put the decision in one place. If the user has a live connection, push it; otherwise, email them:

```js
function notify(user, message) {
    if (online[user.id])
        online[user.id].send(message);            // real-time
    else
        MAIL(user.email, 'New task for you', message);   // offline
}
```

`MAIL(to, subject, body)` is the built-in SMTP client — no package needed. The caller doesn't care which channel was used; `notify` decides.

## Build it in Tasker

Below, user **2** is connected and user **3** isn't. Write `notify(user, message)` so that **online users get a socket push** (use `pushSocket`) and **offline users get an email** (`MAIL`). Then notify both.

challenge.prompt: Complete `notify(user, message)`: if the user is in `online`, push with `pushSocket`; otherwise send with `MAIL`. The online user (2) should get a socket push, the offline user (3) an email.
challenge.hint: if (online[user.id]) pushSocket(user.id, message); else MAIL(user.email, 'New task for you', message);

checks.title: The online user gets a socket push
The offline user gets an email

lessons.title: Reminders on a schedule

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.summary: Look back + other ways with Total.js
lessons.theory:
# Recap

> A calm look back — and the other built-in tools for collaboration, no packages.

## What you built
Tasker is now collaborative: users **assign** tasks to each other and get **notified** — instantly if online, by email if not, and by **scheduled reminders** for what's due.

## What you used
`DB().update(...).where('id', ...)` to assign, a `SOCKET` route with an `online` map and `client.send` for real-time, `MAIL(...)` as the offline fallback, and `CRON(...)` / `SCHEDULE(...)` for reminders.

## Other ways to do it (all built in)
- **Broadcast** — push to *every* connected client at once (e.g. a shared team board).
- **`PUBLISH` / `SUBSCRIBE`** — decouple senders from receivers across the app.
- **`HTMLMAIL`** and mail **views** — send rich, templated emails, not just plain text.
- **`WEBSOCKETCLIENT`** — connect *out* to another server's socket.
- **`TMS` / `TMSCLIENT`** — message between separate Total.js apps.
- **`ON('service', fn)`** — a minute counter for lightweight recurring chores.

## 🔎 What's next
Tasker is feature-complete. Time to harden it for the real world — release mode, bundles and scaling — in **Module 10 · Going to production**. 🚀

lessons.title: Overview

# Module 1 · Your first jComponent UI

> **Goal:** give Tasker a real, interactive face — with **jComponent**, Total.js's client framework.

In the Backend aula you built Tasker's API. Now we build its **UI**. Total.js's frontend is **jComponent**: a component library where the building blocks live **right in your HTML**.

You write a tag like this:

```html
<ui-component name="button">Add task</ui-component>
```

…and jComponent turns it into a real, styled button. No build step, no bundler, no `npm install` — just components in markup, exactly like the zero-dependency spirit of the backend.

In this module you'll render Tasker's header and your first component, and learn how to **configure** components. Everything you write here renders **live** in the preview on the right. 🎨

## 💡 Why jComponent?

There are **300+ ready components** — inputs, checkboxes, date pickers, color pickers, data grids, charts, even a WebSocket client. You rarely touch raw HTML5: you compose components. And it ships with **IntranetCSS**, a big ready-made stylesheet, plus **Tangular** templating. One toolkit for the whole UI.

## Other things you can drop in (all built in)
jComponent has **300+ components** — you compose UIs instead of writing raw HTML:

- **Inputs** — `j-Textbox`, `j-Checkbox`, `j-Dropdown`, `j-Search`, color/date/icon pickers.
- **Layout & feedback** — `j-Repeater` (lists), `j-Empty`, `j-Loading`, `j-Message`, `j-Confirm`.

- Plus **IntranetCSS** (ready styling) and **Tangular** templating.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 2 · Data binding

> **Goal:** connect components to data so the UI updates itself.

A static button is nice, but real apps show **data**. In jComponent every component can bind to a **path** in a plain JavaScript object — your **model**:

```html
<ui-component name="textbox" path="task.title">Title</ui-component>
```

```js
var task = { title: 'Buy milk' };   // the model
```

Type in the textbox and `task.title` updates. Want to *show* a value live? Drop a **`<ui-bind>`**:

```html
<ui-bind path="task.title" config="text"></ui-bind>
```

No `addEventListener`, no manual DOM updates — bind the path once and jComponent keeps both sides in sync. 🔗

## 💡 Why jComponent?
The model is just a normal object — no special `useState`, no observables to wire. Components read and write it by path, and **`<ui-bind>`** / **j-Template** re-render automatically when it changes.

lessons.title: Bind an input to data
lessons.theory:


```js
var task = { title: 'Buy milk' };
```

The textbox starts filled with `Buy milk`, and the `<ui-bind>` echoes it as text. Edit one, the other follows.

lessons.title: Formatting strings & numbers
lessons.theory:
# Formatting strings & numbers

> **Goal:** show numbers and text neatly — no manual string juggling.

## Learn

jComponent's Tangular helpers format values right in the template:

- **`format(n)`** — numbers/dates: `{{ avg | format(2) }}` → `1.50`.
- **`pluralize(zero, one, many)`** — pick the right word for a count: `{{ count | pluralize('No tasks', 'One task', '# tasks') }}` (`#` becomes the number).
- **`empty(fallback)`** — show a fallback when a value is blank: `{{ note | empty('Nothing noted') }}`.
- **`currency`** — money: `{{ total | currency }}`.
- **`encode`** — make user text safe to show as HTML.

One pipe each — the formatting lives in the template, not scattered through your code.

## Build it in Tasker

Render a summary with a **template** bound to `stats`: a `pluralize`d count, an average with `format(2)`, and a note with `empty('Nothing noted')`. The model: count **3**, avg **1.5**, note **empty**.

# Module 3 · Forms & validation

> **Goal:** build Tasker's add-task form — and keep it honest.

Forms are where jComponent shines. Every input is a component with built-in **validation**, so you describe the rules in `config` and the framework enforces them:

```html
<ui-component name="textbox" path="task.title" config="required:true">Title</ui-component>
```

You'll add a **required** title field, a **checkbox**, and a **button** that stays disabled until the form is valid — no manual checks, no scattered `if` statements.

## 💡 Why jComponent?
`j-Textbox`, `j-Checkbox`, `j-Dropdown`, date/colour pickers… every input is a component with **validation, placeholders, icons and types** built in. The button's `validation:1` watches the path and enables itself — the framework wires the logic.

```html
              config="required:true;placeholder:What needs doing?;icon:check">Title</ui-component>
```

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 7 · Talking to the backend

> **Goal:** connect Tasker's UI to the API you built in the Backend aula.

Your jComponent UI is lovely, but its tasks live in the browser. Time to wire it to the **real server** — the Total.js app from the Backend course, with its `API` routes.

jComponent talks to Total.js with two helpers:

- **`TAPI()`** — calls an **API operation** over HTTP (load tasks, create one).
- **`WAPI()`** — the same, but over a **WebSocket**, for live two-way updates.

Remember the backend's `ROUTE('API /api/ -tasks_list *Tasks --> list')`? That's exactly what the UI calls — by name. The two aulas meet here. 🤝

> These lessons read like a guide rather than a runnable exercise: they need the live Total.js server running, which the in-app preview doesn't host. You'll run them against your own Tasker backend.

lessons.title: Load & save with TAPI
Real-time with WAPI
Recap
lessons.theory:
# Recap

## What you connected
Tasker's jComponent UI now talks to the Total.js backend: **`TAPI()`** for HTTP, **`WAPI()`** + **j-WebSocket** for real-time — calling the very `API` operations you built server-side.

## The whole picture
One framework, both ends: schemas and `API` routes on the server, components and binding in the browser, the **same operation names** bridging them. Zero dependencies the whole way down.

## 🔎 Good to know — keeping components local
By default jComponent pulls components from the **official Total.js CDN**. Prefer to keep everything in your own project (offline, no external dependency)? Total.js has **`COMPONENTATOR`** on the server:

```js
COMPONENTATOR('tasker', 'exec,input,checkbox,repeater,empty,websocket', true);
// REPO.tasker -> '/ui-tasker-3487934.js'  (JS + CSS of all listed components)
```

It downloads and bundles the listed UI components into a single local file (regenerated when the list changes), so your app ships its own components — exactly the zero-dependency philosophy of the whole course. 📦

## 🎓 You made it
From `GET /` to a real-time, validated, multi-user task app with a jComponent front end — built on one zero-dependency framework. That's Total.js. 🚀

tracks.title: Module 6 · Real-world jComponent
tracks.summary: The model API, WATCH, events, scopes and navigation.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 6 · Real-world jComponent

> **Goal:** the methods that turn components into a real app.

You can build any screen now. Real apps need a bit more: change data from code, react to changes, let parts of the app talk to each other, and move between pages. jComponent has a small, sharp toolkit for all of it:

- **Model API** — `SET` / `GET` / `UPDATE` / `INC` / `TOGGLE` / `PUSH`.
- **Reactivity** — `WATCH(path, fn)` runs when data changes.
- **Events** — `EMIT(name, data)` / `ON(name, fn)` to decouple parts.
- **Scopes** — isolate a slice of the model for a sub-UI.
- **Navigation** — `j-Page`, the `NAV` object, and `ui-import`.

These are the pieces you reach for daily in production — and the doorway to exploring the rest of jComponent v20. 🔧

## 💡 Why jComponent?
It's all global and tiny: no store library, no event bus to install, no router package. The model *is* a plain object, and these few functions drive everything around it.

lessons.title: The model API
lessons.theory:
# The model API

> **Goal:** change Tasker's data straight from code.

## Learn

The model is a plain object, and a handful of globals drive it from anywhere:

```js
SET('task.title', 'Buy milk');   // set a value
GET('task.title');               // read it
UPDATE('task.title', v => v.trim());  // transform in place
INC('stats.done', 1);            // increment a number
TOGGLE('task.done');             // flip a boolean
PUSH('tasks', { title: 'New task' }); // append to an array
```

Anything bound to those paths (a `<ui-bind>`, a repeater) updates **instantly** — you change data, the UI follows.

## Build it in Tasker

A repeater shows `tasks`. Add a button whose `addTask()` does **`PUSH('tasks', { title: 'New task' })`**. Clicking it should add a row.

lessons.title: Events: EMIT & ON
lessons.theory:
# Events: EMIT & ON

> **Goal:** let parts of the app talk without knowing about each other.

## Learn

Sometimes one piece of UI should react to something that happened elsewhere. Instead of wiring them directly, **broadcast an event** and **listen** for it:

```js
ON('task:shared', function(data) {     // listen
    SET('last', 'Shared: ' + data.title);
});

EMIT('task:shared', { title: 'Buy milk' });  // broadcast
```

Anyone can `ON` an event; anyone can `EMIT` it. The sender doesn't care who's listening — that's how you keep a big app loosely coupled (a notification badge, a logger, a live counter…).

## Build it in Tasker

Listen with **`ON('task:shared', fn)`** and, in the handler, `SET('last', 'Shared: ' + data.title)`. The **Share** button should `EMIT('task:shared', { title: 'Buy milk' })`. Clicking it should show the shared task.

# Working with arrays

> **Goal:** add to and trim a list, and watch the UI follow.

## Learn

Lists live in the model as arrays. A few moves cover most needs:

- **Add** — `PUSH('tasks', { title: 'New task' })` appends an item.
- **Remove / filter** — read, filter, set it back:
  ```js
  SET('tasks', GET('tasks').filter(function(t) { return !t.done; }));
  ```
- **Replace / clear** — `SET('tasks', [])`.

Anything bound to `tasks` (a repeater) re-renders automatically — you change the array, the list updates.

## Build it in Tasker

The list shows `tasks` in a repeater. Make the **Add** button `PUSH` a `{ title: 'New task' }`, and the **Clear done** button keep only the undone ones with `SET` + `GET().filter(...)`. Clicking **Add** should add a row.

lessons.title: Scopes
lessons.summary: Isolate a slice of the model
lessons.theory: # Scopes > **Goal:** understand how jComponent isolates a slice of data for a sub-UI. A **scope** is a region of your UI bound to one part of the model, where inner paths are **relative** to it. You've used scopes already without noticing — every row in a **repeater** is its own scope: ```html ``` Each repeated item is wrapped in an **independent scope with a direct reference to the source array** — change the row, the array updates. Components like `j-Duplicator`, `j-Form` and `j-ListForm` work the same way: they give a block of UI its own scope so the same template can be reused for many items. ## Why it matters Scopes are how you build **lists of editable things** — a list of tasks, a list of form rows — without hand-writing unique paths for each. You write the template once; jComponent scopes it per item. > 🔧 Forms can opt out or customise their scope (`scope: false`, or a custom `scope` name) when you need data to live somewhere specific — handy once your screens get complex.

lessons.title: SPA navigation
lessons.theory:
# SPA navigation

> **Goal:** move between screens without reloading the page.

jComponent apps are **single-page**: you swap screens by changing data, not by loading new HTML.

## Pages with j-Page
A **page** shows or hides based on a value in the model:

```html
<ui-component name="page" path="screen" config="if:list;url:/views/list.html"></ui-component>
<ui-component name="page" path="screen" config="if:detail;url:/views/detail.html"></ui-component>
```

```js
SET('screen', 'list');   // show the list page
SET('screen', 'detail'); // switch to detail
```

The page downloads its `url` the first time it's shown, then caches it. Navigation is just `SET`.

## The NAV object
**`NAV`** is the client router. It exposes the current route's **`params`** and **`query`**, and methods like **`NAV.reload()`** / **`NAV.refresh()`**. Pair it with client-side `ROUTE('/tasks/{id}/', fn)` to react to URL changes — deep links, back/forward, the lot.

## 🔎 Good to know — `ui-import`
Need a component or a block of HTML only sometimes? **`<ui-import>`** loads it lazily, on demand:

```html
<ui-import path="/components/heavy-chart.html"></ui-import>
```

It fetches and compiles the markup the first time it's needed — great for keeping the initial app small while still having everything available. Pairs nicely with **COMPONENTATOR** on the server (from the next module) to keep those imports local.

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.theory:
# Recap

## What you learned
The everyday toolkit: drive the model (`SET`/`GET`/`UPDATE`/`INC`/`TOGGLE`/`PUSH`), react with `WATCH`, decouple with `EMIT`/`ON`, isolate data with **scopes**, and navigate with `j-Page` + `NAV` + `ui-import`.

## More to explore (all built in)
- **`NEWUIBIND`** — invent your own binding directive.
- **`Tangular.register`** — your own template helpers (you did this in Module 2).
- **`CONFIG`**, **`LOCALIZE`** / **`TRANSLATE`** — settings and i18n on the client.
- **`AJAX`**, **`UPLOAD`**, **`IMPORT`** — talk to the network directly.
- **`PLUGIN`** / `ui-plugin` — split a big app into independent plugins.

That's the invitation: jComponent v20 is deep. You now know enough to read the docs and reach for whatever a real screen needs. 🔭

## 🔎 What's next
The finale: connect this UI to the **backend** you built — load and save with `TAPI`, go real-time with `WAPI`. 🔌

tracks.title: Module 5 · Interactivity & state
tracks.summary: Change the model on click and filter live.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 5 · Interactivity & state

> **Goal:** make Tasker *do* things — change data on click, filter as you type.

So far the UI reflects data. Now it **changes** it. The **click** component runs an action or sets a value on the model:

```html
<button><ui-component name="click" path="task.title" config="value:Walk the dog">Set</ui-component></button>
```

Clicking sets `task.title` — and anything bound to it (a `<ui-bind>`, a repeater) updates instantly. You'll also wire a **search** box that filters the list live.

## 💡 Why jComponent?
`SET`, `GET` and `EXEC` are the whole state story. The **click** component sets values or calls functions; **j-Search** filters a selector as you type — no debounce code, no manual filtering.

lessons.title: Change data on click
lessons.theory:
# Change data on click

> **Goal:** run code when a button is clicked, and update the model.

## Learn

Give a **button** an **`exec`** in its config — the name of a function to run on click. Inside it, **`SET(path, value)`** writes to the model, and anything bound to that path updates instantly:

```html
<ui-component name="button" config="exec:setTitle">Pick task</ui-component>
Current: <ui-bind path="task.title" config="text"></ui-bind>
```

```js
var task = { title: '' };
function setTitle() {
    SET('task.title', 'Walk the dog');
}
```

Click the button → `setTitle` runs → `SET` updates `task.title` → the `<ui-bind>` re-renders. No event wiring, no DOM lookups.

## Build it in Tasker

Add a **button** with `config="exec:setTitle"`, a `setTitle()` function that does `SET('task.title', 'Walk the dog')`, and a **`<ui-bind>`** showing the title. Clicking should update it.

# Filter as you type > **Goal:** filter the task list live with a search box. ## Learn A **search** box binds to a path and filters elements by their `data-search` value as you type. Pair it with a `type:search` textbox on the same path: ```html Search
Buy milk
Walk the dog
 ``` ```js var query = ''; ``` Type "milk" and only matching rows stay visible — no filtering code. ## Build it in Tasker Add a `type:search` textbox bound to `query`, and a **search** component (`selector:.task`) wrapping two `data-search` task rows.

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.theory:
# Recap

## What you built
Interactivity: a button that sets data on click, and a live search box that filters the list.

## What you used
The **click** component (`value:` / actions), **`<ui-bind>`** to reflect changes, and **j-Search** with `data-search` + `selector`.

## Other ways to drive state (all built in)
- **`SET('task.title', v)`** / **`GET(...)`** / **`UPD(...)`** — read and write the model from code.
- **`EXEC('Action/name')`** — run a declared action from any component.
- **`j-Click` actions**, **`j-Shortcuts`** (keyboard), **`j-ContextMenu`** — more triggers.
- **`WATCH('task', fn)`** — react to model changes yourself.

## 🔎 What's next
You can build any screen now. Next: the **real-world toolkit** — drive the model from code, react to changes with `WATCH`, events, scopes and navigation. ⚙️

tracks.title: Module 0 · First steps in code
tracks.summary: Never coded before? Start here — the basics, then build for real.

lessons.title: Overview
lessons.theory:
# Module 0 · First steps in code

> **Goal:** start from absolute zero — no experience needed.

Never written code before? Perfect. This module gives you just enough to feel at home, then we build a real app together.

## What is an app?
Every app is three things:

1. **Data** — the information it remembers (your tasks).
2. **Logic** — the rules that do things (add a task, mark it done).
3. **Interface** — what you see and tap (a list, a button).

Across this campus you'll build **Tasker**, a real to-do app:
- The **backend** (the server) stores tasks and answers requests.
- The **frontend** (the screen) shows them and lets you interact.

## Code is just instructions
Code is a set of clear instructions for the computer, written in a language. Ours is **JavaScript** — the language of both Total.js (backend) and jComponent (frontend). Learn it once, use it everywhere.

Ready? Let's write your first line. 👇

lessons.title: Values & variables
lessons.theory:
# Values & variables

> **Goal:** store pieces of information and use them.

## Learn

A **variable** is a labelled box that holds a **value**. You create one with `var`:

```js
var title = 'Buy milk';   // text (a "string")
var priority = 1;         // a number
var done = false;         // true / false (a "boolean")
```

- **Strings** are text, in quotes.
- **Numbers** are just numbers.
- **Booleans** are `true` or `false`.

`console.log(...)` prints a value so you can see it:

```js
console.log(title);   // Buy milk
```

You can join strings with `+`:

```js
console.log(title + ' has priority ' + priority);
```

## Build it in Tasker

Create three variables for a task — a **title** (string), a **priority** (number) and **done** (boolean) — and print a sentence using all three.
lessons.starterCode:
// Describe one task with three variables, then print a sentence.
var title = 'Buy milk';

challenge.prompt: Create `title` (string), `priority` (number) and `done` (boolean), then `console.log` a sentence that includes all three. Use **Buy milk** as the title.
challenge.hint: var priority = 1; var done = false; console.log(title + ' — priority ' + priority + ', done: ' + done);

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.theory:
# Functions

> **Goal:** wrap logic so you can reuse it.

## Learn

A **function** is a named block of instructions. You **define** it once and **call** it whenever you need it:

```js
function describe(title) {
    return 'Task: ' + title;
}

console.log(describe('Buy milk'));   // Task: Buy milk
console.log(describe('Walk the dog')); // Task: Walk the dog
```

- The words in `()` are **inputs** (called *parameters*).
- `return` hands a value back to whoever called the function.

Functions are how apps stay tidy: write the rule once, use it everywhere.

## Build it in Tasker

Write a function `describe(title)` that returns `'Task: ' + title`, then call it with **Buy milk** and print the result.
lessons.starterCode: // Write a function that describes a task, then call it.

challenge.prompt: Define `function describe(title)` that returns `'Task: ' + title`, then `console.log(describe('Buy milk'))`.
challenge.hint: function describe(title) { return 'Task: ' + title; } console.log(describe('Buy milk'));

lessons.title: Lists & records
lessons.theory:
# Lists & records

> **Goal:** hold many things, and group related facts.

## Learn

Two shapes do most of the work in any app:

**An object** groups related facts (a *record*) — one task:

```js
var task = { title: 'Buy milk', done: false };
console.log(task.title);   // Buy milk  (read with a dot)
```

**An array** is an ordered list — many tasks:

```js
var tasks = ['Buy milk', 'Walk the dog'];
```

To do something with every item, use `forEach`:

```js
tasks.forEach(function(task) {
    console.log('- ' + task);
});
```

Tasker is exactly this: a **list (array)** of **tasks (objects)**.

## Build it in Tasker

Make an array of two task objects (each with a `title`), then loop with `forEach` and print every title.

challenge.prompt: Loop over the `tasks` array with `forEach` and `console.log` each task's `title`. Both **Buy milk** and **Walk the dog** should print.

lessons.title: How data travels: JSON
lessons.theory:
# How data travels: JSON

> **Goal:** turn data into text and back — how apps talk.

## Learn

When a frontend and a backend talk over the network, they can't send a live object — they send **text**. The standard format is **JSON**, and it looks just like a JavaScript object.

- **`JSON.stringify(obj)`** turns an object into JSON text (to send it).
- **`JSON.parse(text)`** turns JSON text back into an object (to use it).

```js
var task = { title: 'Buy milk', done: false };

var text = JSON.stringify(task);   // '{"title":"Buy milk","done":false}'
console.log(text);

var back = JSON.parse(text);
console.log(back.title);           // Buy milk
```

Every request your Tasker backend answers will travel as JSON. You just met the language of the web. 🌐

## Build it in Tasker

Turn a `task` object into JSON text with `JSON.stringify`, print it, then `JSON.parse` it back and print its `title`.

challenge.prompt: Use `JSON.stringify(task)` to print the task as text, then `JSON.parse(...)` it back and print its `title`.
challenge.hint: var text = JSON.stringify(task); console.log(text); var back = JSON.parse(text); console.log(back.title);

checks.title: The title survives the round trip

lessons.title: What is a server?
lessons.theory:
# What is a server?

> **Goal:** understand how the web works, in one picture.

## The whole web, simplified
The web is a conversation between two sides:

- A **client** (a browser, a phone app) **asks** for something — a *request*.
- A **server** (a program running somewhere) **answers** — a *response*.

```
Client:  "GET  /tasks"          →   (please give me the tasks)
Server:  200 OK + [ ...tasks ]  →   (here they are, as JSON)
```

That's it. Every app you use is millions of these little request → response trips.

## Where Total.js fits
Tasker's **backend** is a Total.js **server**: it waits for requests like `GET /tasks` or `POST /tasks`, runs your logic, and answers — usually with **JSON** (the text format you just learned).

- **GET** — "give me something."
- **POST** — "here's something new, save it."
- **PUT** — "update this." · **DELETE** — "remove this."

In the next module you'll start your very own server and make it answer its first request. You're ready. 🚀

lessons.title: Recap
lessons.theory:
# Recap

## What you learned
The building blocks of all code: **values** (strings, numbers, booleans), **functions** (reusable logic), **objects & arrays** (records and lists), **JSON** (how data travels), and what a **server** does (request → response).

That's genuinely everything you need to start. Total.js takes it from here.

## 🛠️ Build it for real (recommended)
This campus runs code safely inside the app, which is perfect for learning. To make it *stick*, build along in parallel:

- Open your favourite editor — or **Total.js Code**, the official Total.js editor.
- Create a real Tasker project and type the code as you go through the modules.
- The **finished Tasker** and the **importable language packs** live in our public Git repository, so you can compare, get unstuck, and keep them as a reference.

You learn to code by *coding*. Use the campus to understand, your editor to build. 💪

## 🔎 What's next
**Module 1 · Getting started** — install Total.js and bring Tasker's server to life with its first endpoint.
